tests.title: IELTS Academic Reading — Test 02
tests.description: Original full-length Academic Reading test: 3 passages, 40 questions, 60 minutes.

sections.title: Passage 1 — The Reinvented Library
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1–13.
sections.passage:
The Reinvented Library

¶1 When affordable home computers and the internet arrived in the 1990s, many commentators confidently predicted the end of the public library. If almost any fact could be summoned instantly to a screen, what use was a building full of paper? Yet the obituaries proved premature. In a great many cities, library visits have risen rather than fallen over the past two decades, and the institution has survived by quietly redefining what a library is for. The challenge it answered, librarians like to point out, was not how to compete with the internet but how to become something the internet could not replace. A screen could deliver facts; it could not, on its own, teach a nervous adult how to use it, nor offer a warm and unjudging place to sit.

¶2 At the heart of this reinvention lies an old idea given fresh emphasis: the library as a third place. Sociologists use that phrase for the spaces in our lives that are neither home nor work, where people may gather freely and without obligation. Cafés and parks are third places too, but they usually expect a visitor to spend money or move along. A library asks for nothing. It is one of the very few remaining public buildings a person may enter, stay in for hours, and leave again without being required to buy a thing. In an age when so much of public life has migrated to private, paywalled or advertising-funded platforms, that simple openness has come to seem quietly radical.

¶3 Around this principle libraries have layered services that would once have seemed to have nothing to do with books. Many now provide free internet access and classes in basic computing, alongside meeting rooms that community groups may book at no cost. A growing number house so-called maker spaces, equipped with tools such as 3D printers and sewing machines that few households could justify buying for themselves. The lending of objects has expanded too, from board games to musical instruments, on exactly the principle that once underpinned the lending of books: shared access to things that are useful but rarely needed. A power drill or a telescope sits idle in most homes for all but a few hours a year, and libraries have begun to ask why such items should be owned at all rather than shared.

¶4 These services matter most to those who have least. For a jobseeker without a computer at home, the library may be the only place to write an application or sit an online interview. For an elderly resident baffled by a new government website, a librarian's patient help can be the difference between receiving a benefit and missing out on it altogether. By offering such support free of charge, libraries have quietly become one of society's main defences against the digital divide, the gap between those who can confidently use technology and those who cannot. The point is easily missed by people for whom devices are second nature, but for millions that gap is real and widening.

¶5 The buildings themselves have changed to suit their new role. The hushed, forbidding reading rooms of the past have given way to open, flexible interiors flooded with natural light, where quiet conversation is tolerated and even encouraged. Some include cafés, performance areas or rooftop gardens. Architects increasingly treat a new library as a civic statement, a way for a city to declare what it values, much as cathedrals once announced the priorities of an earlier age. The shift is not merely cosmetic: a welcoming, legible building draws in people who might once have found a library intimidating, and the design itself signals that everyone is expected and everyone belongs.

¶6 None of this guarantees the library's survival. In periods of austerity, councils hunting for savings often regard libraries as soft targets, since closing one provokes less immediate outcry than cutting a hospital or a school. Hundreds of branches have shut in some countries, and others continue only because volunteers have stepped in to replace paid, professional staff. Critics warn that a service kept alive by goodwill alone is fragile, and that the slow erosion of expertise may prove far harder to reverse than a sudden closure. Once a trained librarian leaves, the accumulated knowledge of a place and its readers is not easily rebuilt, however willing the volunteers who follow.

¶7 Defenders of the library argue that its worth simply cannot be captured by counting how many books are borrowed. A modern library lends objects, teaches skills, shelters the vulnerable and provides a rare space that belongs to everyone and to no one in particular. To judge it by loan figures alone, they suggest, is like judging a park by the number of people who walk straight across it without stopping. As cities grow denser and genuinely public space becomes ever scarcer, the case for the library as essential civic infrastructure may be stronger today than at any point since it was first invented.

questions.text: In the 1990s some commentators expected public libraries to disappear.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says many commentators 'confidently predicted the end of the public library', so the statement is supported.

evidence.quote: confidently predicted the end of the public library

questions.text: Library visits have fallen steadily over the past twenty years.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says visits 'have risen rather than fallen over the past two decades', contradicting the claim of a steady fall.

evidence.quote: have risen rather than fallen over the past two decades

questions.text: Unlike a café, a library does not expect visitors to spend money.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 contrasts cafés, which expect spending, with the library, which 'asks for nothing' and lets people stay 'without being required to buy a thing'.

evidence.quote: without being required to buy a thing

questions.text: Most households already own tools such as 3D printers.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says maker spaces hold tools 'that few households could justify buying for themselves', so 'most households already own' them is false.

evidence.quote: that few households could justify buying for themselves

questions.text: Library staff numbers have increased in every country.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 mentions branch closures and volunteers replacing staff in 'some countries', but makes no claim about staff numbers in every country.

questions.text: Sociologists describe a space that is neither home nor work as a ______ place.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 introduces 'the library as a third place', defined as neither home nor work.

evidence.quote: the library as a third place

questions.text: Libraries help close the digital ______, the gap between those who can use technology and those who cannot.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 calls libraries a defence against 'the digital divide, the gap between those who can confidently use technology and those who cannot'.

evidence.quote: the digital divide

questions.text: Architects increasingly treat a new library building as a civic ______.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says architects 'treat a new library as a civic statement'.

evidence.quote: treat a new library as a civic statement

questions.text: Which paragraph describes the kinds of objects, besides books, that libraries now lend?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 lists lending 'from board games to musical instruments'.

evidence.quote: from board games to musical instruments

questions.text: Which paragraph explains why councils may see libraries as easy to cut?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says councils regard libraries as 'soft targets' because closures cause less outcry than cutting a hospital or school.

evidence.quote: regard libraries as soft targets

questions.text: Which paragraph compares modern library buildings to cathedrals?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says a library declares a city's values 'much as cathedrals once announced the priorities of an earlier age'.

evidence.quote: much as cathedrals once announced the priorities of an earlier age

questions.text: Which paragraph argues that loan figures are an inadequate measure of a library's worth?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says a library's worth 'cannot be captured by counting how many books are borrowed'.

evidence.quote: cannot be captured by counting how many books are borrowed

questions.text: According to Paragraph 6, who often keeps threatened branches open by replacing professional staff?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says some branches survive 'only because volunteers have stepped in to replace paid, professional staff'.

evidence.quote: volunteers have stepped in to replace paid, professional staff

sections.title: Passage 2 — Tracking the Invisible Migrants
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 14–26.
sections.passage:
Tracking the Invisible Migrants

¶1 For as long as people have watched the skies, the seasonal disappearance and return of birds has been a source of wonder and bafflement. Some ancient writers supposed that swallows spent the winter buried in the mud at the bottom of ponds; others imagined that birds flew to the moon and back. Even after migration was finally accepted as fact, its details remained stubbornly hidden, for the simple reason that a small animal which vanishes over the horizon is almost impossible to follow. For centuries, in consequence, the study of migration advanced at the slow pace of patient guesswork, and much of what passed for knowledge was little more than informed speculation. The very regularity of the birds' comings and goings, so dependable that farmers once timed their planting by them, only sharpened the central puzzle of where the travellers actually went once they had passed out of sight.

¶2 The first genuine breakthrough was bird ringing, also called banding, in which a lightweight numbered ring is fixed to a bird's leg in the hope that the bird will later be found somewhere else. The method confirmed beyond doubt that individual birds travelled enormous distances, and it remains useful today. Yet it depended entirely on the slim chance of a ringed bird being recovered and reported, and for every thousand rings fitted only a handful ever yielded any information at all. Crucially, ringing could reveal a journey's starting point and its end, but almost nothing about the route in between, the speed of travel, or the places where a bird paused along the way. For a long time, in short, ringing told researchers that migration happened without ever telling them quite how.

¶3 Electronics transformed the field. As batteries and transmitters grew smaller and lighter, it became possible to fit animals with devices that report their position directly rather than waiting for a chance recovery. The earliest satellite tags were heavy enough to be carried only by large creatures such as albatrosses, swans and whales. Within a few decades, however, the same essential technology had been shrunk to a few grams, light enough for a modest songbird to carry without obvious harm. For the first time, researchers sitting at a computer could watch an individual animal's movements unfold across a map almost as they happened, week by week and sometimes day by day. A journey that had once taken a lifetime of patient fieldwork to infer could now be sketched in the course of a single season.

¶4 The discoveries that followed overturned assumptions that had stood for generations. A shorebird called the bar-tailed godwit was found to fly from Alaska to New Zealand without once stopping to rest or feed, a journey of more than eleven thousand kilometres completed in roughly nine days of continuous flight. Other species proved to follow great looping routes, travelling south by one path and returning north by an entirely different one, each leg chosen to make use of the prevailing winds of the season. Such feats had been not merely unknown but literally unimaginable while the birds in question could not be followed through the air. Each new track seemed to reveal a navigational feat more astonishing than the one before it.

¶5 For the very smallest migrants, even a few grams of equipment remain too heavy a burden, and for these a far cheaper device known as a light-level geolocator has proved invaluable. Rather than transmitting a signal, the geolocator simply records the changing level of daylight; from the timing of sunrise and sunset, calculated afterwards, a bird's approximate position on each day of its journey can be reconstructed. The drawback is considerable. Because the data are stored on the tag itself rather than sent to a satellite, the bird has to be physically caught a second time so that the device can be removed and read. A great deal therefore depends on the same individual returning, a year later, to the very nesting site where it was first captured.

¶6 The newest tags do far more than record where an animal has been. Tiny accelerometers can detect the individual beat of a bird's wings, or the precise moment it touches down; other sensors log temperature, altitude and even heart rate throughout the journey. Combined, these streams of information allow researchers to reconstruct not only where an animal travelled but what it was doing at each stage, how hard its body was working, and the conditions it had to endure. A migration that an earlier generation could represent only as two dots on a map can now be read almost minute by minute, like a diary written by the animal itself.

¶7 The practical value of all this for conservation is considerable. By revealing exactly where migrating animals pause to rest and refuel, tracking has identified critical stopover sites whose importance no one had previously suspected, making it possible to protect them before they are lost to farming or development. Yet the technology raises difficult questions of its own. A tag that is even slightly too heavy may change the very behaviour it was meant to record; the equipment remains costly, which can skew research towards a handful of well-funded species; and the sheer flood of data now being gathered far outstrips the capacity of scientists to make sense of it. The invisible migrants have at last been made visible, but seeing them clearly has brought new responsibilities as well as new knowledge. Conservationists long accustomed to working with mere scraps of evidence suddenly faced the unfamiliar difficulty of holding more information than they could properly use.

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 1
options: A first method with serious limits
How shrinking electronics changed the field
Journeys no one had thought possible
A cheap tool with an awkward catch
Reading an animal's journey in detail
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 describes how migration's details stayed hidden and study 'advanced at the slow pace of patient guesswork'.

evidence.quote: advanced at the slow pace of patient guesswork

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 2
options: A first method with serious limits
How shrinking electronics changed the field
Journeys no one had thought possible
A cheap tool with an awkward catch
Reading an animal's journey in detail
correct_answer: A first method with serious limits
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains that ringing worked but 'depended entirely on the slim chance of a ringed bird being recovered' and revealed little about the route.

evidence.quote: depended entirely on the slim chance of a ringed bird being recovered

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 3
options: A first method with serious limits
How shrinking electronics changed the field
Journeys no one had thought possible
A cheap tool with an awkward catch
Reading an animal's journey in detail
correct_answer: How shrinking electronics changed the field
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 describes how, as transmitters 'grew smaller and lighter', tags could be fitted even to songbirds.

evidence.quote: grew smaller and lighter

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 4
options: A first method with serious limits
How shrinking electronics changed the field
Journeys no one had thought possible
A cheap tool with an awkward catch
Reading an animal's journey in detail
correct_answer: Journeys no one had thought possible
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 reports feats such as the godwit's non-stop flight that 'had been not merely unknown but literally unimaginable'.

evidence.quote: not merely unknown but literally unimaginable

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 5
options: A first method with serious limits
How shrinking electronics changed the field
Journeys no one had thought possible
A cheap tool with an awkward catch
Reading an animal's journey in detail
correct_answer: A cheap tool with an awkward catch
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 introduces the cheaper geolocator but notes the bird 'has to be physically caught a second time' to read it.

evidence.quote: the bird has to be physically caught a second time

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 6
options: A first method with serious limits
How shrinking electronics changed the field
Journeys no one had thought possible
A cheap tool with an awkward catch
Reading an animal's journey in detail
correct_answer: Reading an animal's journey in detail
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 explains that modern sensors let a journey 'be read almost minute by minute, like a diary written by the animal itself'.

evidence.quote: read almost minute by minute

questions.text: Some ancient writers believed that swallows spent the winter
options: buried in mud at the bottom of ponds
correct_answer: buried in mud at the bottom of ponds
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says some supposed swallows 'spent the winter buried in the mud at the bottom of ponds'.

evidence.quote: buried in the mud at the bottom of ponds

questions.text: Compared with later tracking methods, bird ringing could mainly reveal
options: a journey's start and end points
correct_answer: a journey's start and end points
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says ringing could reveal 'a journey's starting point and its end, but almost nothing about the route in between'.

evidence.quote: a journey's starting point and its end, but almost nothing about the route in between

questions.text: The earliest satellite tags could be carried only by
options: large creatures such as albatrosses and whales
correct_answer: large creatures such as albatrosses and whales
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says the earliest tags were heavy enough only for 'large creatures such as albatrosses, swans and whales'.

evidence.quote: large creatures such as albatrosses, swans and whales

questions.text: Why do some migrating birds take different routes north and south?
options: to make use of seasonal winds
correct_answer: to make use of seasonal winds
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says each leg of a looping route is 'chosen to make use of the prevailing winds of the season'.

evidence.quote: to make use of the prevailing winds of the season

questions.text: A light-level geolocator works by recording the changing level of ______.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says the geolocator 'simply records the changing level of daylight'.

evidence.quote: records the changing level of daylight

questions.text: Tracking has revealed important ______ sites where migrating animals rest and refuel.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says tracking 'has identified critical stopover sites' previously unsuspected.

evidence.quote: identified critical stopover sites

questions.text: Which TWO problems with tracking technology does the final paragraph mention?
options: tags may change an animal's behaviour
tags are now too light to attach
correct_answer: tags may change an animal's behaviour
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 warns a tag 'may change the very behaviour it was meant to record' and that 'the equipment remains costly'.

evidence.quote: may change the very behaviour it was meant to record
the equipment remains costly

sections.title: Passage 3 — The Limits of the Rational Mind
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 27–40.
sections.passage:
The Limits of the Rational Mind

¶1 For most of its history, economics rested on a flattering assumption about human nature. People were modelled as rational agents who weighed the costs and benefits of every choice and reliably selected whatever best served their interests. This imaginary figure, sometimes called economic man, never acted on impulse, never miscalculated and was never swayed by how a decision happened to be presented. The model was elegant and mathematically convenient, and for a long time its obvious distance from real human behaviour was treated as a minor inconvenience rather than a fatal flaw. Critics who pointed out that real people are forgetful, emotional and inconsistent were told, in effect, that the model described how a perfectly sensible person ought to behave, even if no such person had ever actually existed.

¶2 From the 1970s onwards, a series of experiments began to dismantle this picture. Psychologists studying how people actually make decisions found that the mind relies heavily on mental shortcuts, or heuristics, which usually work well enough but produce predictable errors in particular situations. These errors were not random noise of the kind that cancels out across a population; they leaned consistently in the same direction, which meant they could be measured, reproduced in the laboratory, and even predicted in advance. The discovery that mistakes were systematic, rather than merely careless, is what made a genuine science of them possible. What had looked from a distance like a thousand unconnected lapses turned out, on closer inspection, to share a hidden structure.

¶3 One influential way of organising these findings distinguishes between two modes of thinking. The first is fast, automatic and effortless: it lets us read a word, recognise a face or sense hostility in a voice without any feeling of trying. The second is slow, deliberate and demanding, the kind of attention required to compare two mortgages or check an unfamiliar calculation. Much of the trouble arises because the fast system runs continuously and supplies an immediate answer to almost any question, while the slow system, which could catch its errors, is lazy and easily distracted. We frequently accept the fast system's first suggestion without ever subjecting it to a second look. The two systems are not separate organs lodged somewhere in the brain but simply a convenient way of describing two very different styles of mental work, one cheap and quick, the other costly and slow.

¶4 Some of the resulting biases have become well known. In anchoring, an arbitrary number that a person has just seen influences an unrelated estimate they are then asked to make. In loss aversion, the pain of losing a sum is felt more sharply than the pleasure of gaining the same amount, which makes people reluctant to take even favourable risks. In the availability effect, events that are easy to recall, because they are recent or vivid, are judged to be more common than they really are, so that a single dramatic news story can distort an entire population's sense of what is dangerous. Taken together, these biases show that judgement is shaped not only by the facts of a situation but by entirely irrelevant details of the way those facts happen to arrive.

¶5 If choices are shaped so strongly by context, then the way options are arranged is never neutral, and this insight has been put to practical use. Professor Ines Varga has argued that small changes to the manner in which choices are presented, what she calls the architecture of a decision, can steer behaviour without forbidding any option or altering anyone's incentives. Making enrolment in a pension scheme the default, for instance, while leaving people entirely free to opt out, has been shown to raise participation dramatically. Supporters present such measures as a gentle and inexpensive way to help people act in their own long-term interest. The approach has since been taken up by governments around the world, often through small specialist teams set up expressly to apply it to the design of public policy.

¶6 Not everyone is reassured. Dr Samuel Okonkwo cautions that the same techniques which can nudge a person towards saving can just as easily be turned towards the interests of whoever designs the choice, and that an influence the chooser never notices is difficult to resist or hold to account. There is a further worry. Some of the most celebrated laboratory findings have proved harder to reproduce than was once assumed, and effects that appear robust in a controlled experiment can shrink or vanish in the untidy conditions of real life. Enthusiasm, the critics suggest, has at times run ahead of the evidence. The argument has made the field markedly more cautious, and arguably more honest, about how much weight a single striking result can really be asked to bear.

¶7 What, then, survives the argument? Few researchers now believe that people are simply irrational, a word that implies mere chaos. The emerging consensus is subtler: human beings are boundedly rational, doing the best they can with limited time, limited attention and a brain shaped by evolution for swift judgement rather than careful arithmetic. Understood in this light, the biases are not embarrassing defects to be scolded away but predictable features of a generally effective system. The practical task is neither to mock human reasoning nor to romanticise it, but to design schools, public services and financial products that work with the mind as it actually is, rather than as the old models so conveniently assumed it to be. Seen in this way, the long quarrel between those who place their faith in human reasoning and those who despair of it begins to look like a false choice, and a more useful question comes into view: not whether people are clever or foolish, but how the world around them might be arranged to bring out their better judgements.

questions.text: Traditional economics assumed that people always chose what best served their interests.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says people were modelled as rational agents who 'reliably selected whatever best served their interests'.

evidence.quote: reliably selected whatever best served their interests

questions.text: The decision errors found by psychologists occurred at random.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says the errors 'were not random noise' but 'leaned consistently in the same direction', contradicting the statement.

evidence.quote: leaned consistently in the same direction

questions.text: The fast mode of thinking requires conscious effort.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 describes the first mode as 'fast, automatic and effortless', so saying it requires conscious effort is false.

evidence.quote: fast, automatic and effortless

questions.text: Behavioural research is now taught in most economics degrees.
questions.explanation: The passage discusses the research but never states whether it is taught in economics degrees, so this is Not Given.

questions.text: The writer believes people are simply irrational.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says few researchers believe people are 'simply irrational' and that humans are instead 'boundedly rational', so this contradicts the writer's view.

evidence.quote: human beings are boundedly rational

questions.text: The writer concludes that systems should be designed to fit how the mind really works.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 ends by urging that we design services 'that work with the mind as it actually is'.

evidence.quote: work with the mind as it actually is

questions.text: The imaginary fully rational decision-maker was sometimes called
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 names the figure 'economic man'.

evidence.quote: sometimes called economic man

questions.text: According to the passage, the slow system often fails to catch errors because it is
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says the slow system 'is lazy and easily distracted', so it lets the fast system's answers through.

evidence.quote: is lazy and easily distracted

questions.text: In the availability effect, people overestimate how common an event is when it is
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 explains that events 'easy to recall' are judged more common than they are.

evidence.quote: events that are easy to recall

questions.text: Making pension enrolment the default, while letting people opt out, was shown to
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says using a default for pension enrolment 'has been shown to raise participation dramatically'.

evidence.quote: raise participation dramatically

questions.text: Arranging how choices are presented can steer behaviour without removing any option.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5: Varga argues changes to the 'architecture of a decision' steer behaviour 'without forbidding any option'.

evidence.quote: without forbidding any option or altering anyone's incentives

questions.text: An influence that the chooser never notices is hard to resist or hold to account.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6: Okonkwo cautions that 'an influence the chooser never notices is difficult to resist or hold to account'.

evidence.quote: an influence the chooser never notices is difficult to resist or hold to account

questions.text: What word describes the bias in which a sum lost feels worse than the same sum gained?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 names 'loss aversion', where losing is felt more sharply than gaining the same amount.

evidence.quote: In loss aversion, the pain of losing a sum is felt more sharply

questions.text: Some celebrated laboratory findings have proved harder to ______ than was once assumed.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says some findings 'have proved harder to reproduce than was once assumed'.

evidence.quote: harder to reproduce than was once assumed
